elements.sent-2411-001: No formula that describes the set of P-positions of Amalgamation Nim is known.
elements.sent-2411-002: The author gives a condition on the amalgamation of two piles.
elements.sent-2411-003: Players can amalgamate two piles only when the number of stones in these two piles is equal to or more than 2.
elements.sent-2411-004: Then, we get a formula that describes the set of P-positions for this variant of amalgamation Nim.
elements.sent-2411-005: We denoted the sets of non-negative integers and natural numbers by $\mathbb{Z _{\ge 0}$ and $\mathbb{N}$, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-006: The winning strategy for the game of Nim was determined in 1902 by Bouton \cite{bouton .}
elements.sent-2411-007: Since then, there have been many variants of Nim.
elements.sent-2411-008: Nim and its variants belong to combinatorial games.
elements.sent-2411-009: For a general introduction to combinatorial games, see \cite{lesson .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Amalgamation Nim is a relatively new variant of Nim.
elements.sent-2411-011: One origin of Amalgamation Nim was a query \cite{richard from a student of S.C.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Locke, who is one of the authors of \cite{amalnim .}
elements.sent-2411-013: In this article, we Maximum Amalgamation Nim with restrictions, which is a variant of Amalgamation Nim.
elements.sent-2411-014: In Definition \ref{defofalma , we define Amalgamation Nim.}
elements.sent-2411-015: The two players begin with several piles of stones, and then alternate moves, with the player who makes the last legal move being the winner.
elements.sent-2411-016: The moves are of two types: $(i)$ A player chooses a pile, and removes any number of stones of the pile; $(ii)$ A player can replace two non-empty piles by a single pile containing the total number of stones in the original two piles.
elements.sent-2411-017: We deal with impartial games with no draws; therefore, there are only two outcome classes.
elements.sent-2411-018: For any position $\mathbf{p}$ in game $\mathbf{G}$, a set of positions can be reached by a single move in $\mathbf{G}$, which we denote as move$(\mathbf{p )$.}
elements.sent-2411-019: One of the most important topics of impartial games is to determine the set of $\mathcal{P}$-position.
elements.sent-2411-020: We denote a position of the game by $(x,y,z)$, where $x,y,z$ are the number of stones in the first, the second, and the third pile.
elements.sent-2411-021: For two-pile game of Amalgamation Nim in Definition \ref{defofalma , the set of $\mathcal{P}$-positions is $\{(x,y):x=y\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Therefore, Amalgamation Nim is the same as traditional Nim for two-pile game.
elements.sent-2411-023: When the number of piles is larger than two, there is no formula that describes $\mathcal{P}$-positions of the game in Definition \ref{defofalma .}
elements.sent-2411-024: However, in \cite{amalnim , there is a list of P-positions for the three-heap Nim with one heap of height at most seven.}
elements.sent-2411-025: We define an Amalgamation Nim with Restriction.
elements.sent-2411-026: This is a variant of Amalgamation Nim in which there is a restriction on the number of stones when we merge two piles.
elements.sent-2411-027: Two players take turns to do one of the following $(i)$ and $(ii)$.
elements.sent-2411-028: For $x,y,z \in \mathbb{Z _{\ge 0}$, we define \begin{equation}move(x,y,z)= \{(u,y,z):u \in Z_{\geq0}  and  u<x \}\label{movex} \ \ =\{(x,v,z):v \in Z_{\geq0}  and  v <y\}\label{movey} \ \ =\{(x,y,w):w \in Z_{\geq0}  and  w <z\} \label{movez} \ \ =\{(x+y,0,z),(0,x+y,z): if  x,y \geq 2\} \label{movemerge1} \ \ =\{(x,y+z,0),(x,0,y+z): if  y,z \geq 2\} \label{movemerge2} \ \ =\{(x+z,y,0),(0,y,x+z): if  x,z \geq 2\}. \label{movemerge3}\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}move(x,y,z)= \{(u,y,z):u \in Z_{\geq0}  and  u<x \}\label{movex} \ \ =\{(x,v,z):v \in Z_{\geq0}  and  v <y\}\label{movey} \ \ =\{(x,y,w):w \in Z_{\geq0}  and  w <z\} \label{movez} \ \ =\{(x+y,0,z),(0,x+y,z): if  x,y \geq 2\} \label{movemerge1} \ \ =\{(x,y+z,0),(x,0,y+z): if  y,z \geq 2\} \label{movemerge2} \ \ =\{(x+z,y,0),(0,y,x+z): if  x,z \geq 2\}. \label{movemerge3}\end{equation}
